common_name: Butterfly Laelia (Christmas Orchid, Mexican Laelia, Two-Keeled Laelia)

planting.note: Grow mounted on cork or rough bark, or in a clay pot with very coarse bark or rock chips — Laelia anceps comes from seasonally dry, high-elevation rocky cliffs and oak forests in Mexico and requires excellent drainage and a dry rest in summer after blooming to trigger its characteristic winter bloom in December–January. In zone 9, it is one of the most reliably outdoor-grown Mexican orchids, tolerating brief temperatures to 28°F when the pseudobulbs and medium are dry. High light (bright indirect to partial direct sun) and excellent air circulation are essential. Bloom spikes emerge in fall; flowers open December–January just in time for the holidays. Fragrant in warm conditions.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to partial direct light mounted on bark or in a clay pot with very coarse drainage medium — Butterfly Laelia demands outstanding drainage and air circulation around its roots and performs best with a pronounced dry-warm rest period in the summer months following bloom. Laelia anceps is a sympodial, epiphytic to lithophytic orchid native to Mexico (Oaxaca, Veracruz, Chiapas, Hidalgo) and Honduras in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most cold-tolerant, widely cultivated, and historically important of the Mexican Laelia species and a cornerstone of classic orchid hybridizing. The plant produces cylindrical to spindle-shaped pseudobulbs 3–5 inches tall, each topped with 1–2 stiff, dark-green, leathery, strap-shaped leaves. In late fall, an exceptionally long (to 3 feet) arching flower spike emerges from the apex of the mature pseudobulb, bearing 3–5 (occasionally to 9) large flowers at its tip. Each flower is 3–4 inches across, in shades of soft rose-pink to pale lavender-pink, with a prominent, trumpet-shaped, three-lobed lip in deeper rose to magenta-purple with a white to cream base — the combination giving the flower a dignified, formal beauty that made Laelia anceps one of the most cherished orchid species in Victorian-era collections. Blooming reliably in December and January, the 'Christmas Orchid' provides a display of tropical elegance at a season when few other orchids flower. Fragrant on warm, sunny days. One of the finest Mexican orchids for zone 9–11 outdoor culture on protected, frost-free porches or trees. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Laelia anceps cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Laelia anceps species profile
Royal Horticultural Society — Laelia anceps: culture notes for Mexican orchids
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Laelia (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Giant Sword Fern (Macho Fern, Coarse Sword Fern, Florida Sword Fern)

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Nephrolepis biserrata is the largest of the North American sword ferns, producing arching fronds 3–6 feet long that make a dramatic statement as a bold specimen or mass planting under trees. In zone 9, established plants cut back in freezes but regrow vigorously from the rhizomes in spring. Once established, this fern is remarkably drought tolerant for its size. Excellent for naturalizing under live oaks, palms, and other mature shade trees where its spreading, naturalistic clumps fill large areas with little care. A Florida native of genuine ecological value — larval host for fern-feeding insects. Non-toxic.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — Giant Sword Fern grows most luxuriantly in the dappled shade under large trees where it receives consistent moisture and protection from direct afternoon sun, though it also adapts to brighter light if kept adequately watered. Nephrolepis biserrata is a large, rhizomatous, evergreen fern native to South Florida, the Caribbean, Mexico, Central and South America, tropical Africa, Asia, and the Pacific Islands in the Nephrolepidaceae family — a member of the sword fern genus that includes the Boston Fern (N. exaltata) and Tuberous Sword Fern (N. cordifolia), but considerably larger than either. The plant produces dense, spreading colonies of upright to arching, pinnate fronds 3–6 feet long with dark-green, broadly oblong pinnae (leaflets) that are coarsely toothed (biserrate) along the margins — the texture is coarser and bolder than the common Boston Fern. In Florida, Nephrolepis biserrata grows naturally as both a terrestrial and epiphytic fern in wet hammocks, cypress swamps, and on the trunks of large trees, often forming large, dramatic, cascading masses. As a landscape plant, it is unmatched for dramatic effect in large, shaded areas — a single established clump can spread to 6 feet across, filling substantial areas under oaks and palms with a bold, green tropical texture. One of the finest bold-texture shade plants for zone 9b-12 Gulf Coast landscapes. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Nephrolepis biserrata: Giant Sword Fern for Florida landscapes
Florida Native Plant Society — Nephrolepis biserrata species profile
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Nephrolepis biserrata
University of Florida Herbarium — Nephrolepis biserrata: Florida distribution and ecology

common_name: Blue Taro (Purple Taro, Violet-Stemmed Taro, Black Taro)

planting.note: Plant corms or divisions in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to consistently moist soil; Xanthosoma violaceum tolerates more shade than most edible aroids and performs well under high canopy in zone 9-12, making it particularly useful for shaded food gardens where few edible plants thrive. The striking dark purple leaf stems (petioles) and blue-green, slightly glaucous leaf blades make this a highly ornamental edible. The corms are edible when cooked (similar to taro) and are consumed throughout the Caribbean and Pacific. In zone 9, plants cut back in frost but regrow vigorously from corms in spring. CAUTION: all Xanthosoma parts contain calcium oxalate crystals and must be thoroughly cooked before eating — raw consumption causes severe mouth irritation.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil — Blue Taro grows vigorously in a range of light conditions and is notably more shade-tolerant than many edible root crops, making it a valuable choice for partially shaded food gardens in zone 8b-12. Xanthosoma violaceum is a large, corm-forming, rhizomatous perennial aroid native to the Caribbean and tropical Americas in the Araceae family — closely related to the Dasheen/Taro group (Colocasia) but in the distinct genus Xanthosoma, which tends to have larger, lighter-colored leaf undersides and edible corms with a different starch profile. The plant is most immediately distinguished by its spectacular petioles (leaf stems) in very deep purple-violet to almost black — a color so intense it verges on the appearance of metal or wood rather than a plant part. The large, sagittate (arrowhead-shaped) leaf blades are blue-green with a slightly waxy, glaucous surface. The overall effect is of a large, bold-textured tropical plant of great ornamental impact. The corms (and young leaves, when properly prepared) are used throughout the Caribbean, West Africa, and Pacific Islands as a starchy vegetable similar to taro. Both the corms and young leaves must be thoroughly cooked to neutralize calcium oxalate crystals. The plant provides excellent large-scale ground cover in shaded areas and combines dramatically with lighter-textured shade plants. CAUTION: toxic if eaten raw; cook thoroughly.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Xanthosoma violaceum (Blue Taro) for Florida gardens
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Xanthosoma species: edible aroids for the tropics
LSU AgCenter — Edible Aroids for Gulf Coast Louisiana gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Xanthosoma: edible elephant ears for zone 9

common_name: Wedelia (Beach Creeper, Bay Biscayne Creeper, Orange Wedelia)

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings (rooted or unrooted) in full sun in any well-drained soil; Sphagneticola trilobata (formerly Wedelia trilobata) is one of the most vigorous and fast-spreading groundcovers available for zone 9-12, rooting at stem nodes and spreading to cover large areas within a single season. Excellent for slopes, coastal areas, and difficult-to-maintain areas where dense, weed-suppressing coverage is desired. The cheerful orange-yellow daisy flowers are produced almost continuously through the warm months. In zone 9, established plants cut back in hard freezes but regrow from the root crown vigorously in spring. Classified as invasive in some jurisdictions — check local regulations before planting, and do not plant near natural areas or waterways.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained soil — Wedelia establishes and spreads rapidly in full sun and is one of the most drought-tolerant, low-maintenance groundcovers for warm-climate landscapes once the initial establishment period is complete. Sphagneticola trilobata (formerly Wedelia trilobata, Wedelia paludosa) is a vigorous, mat-forming, spreading perennial in the Asteraceae family native to the Caribbean, Mexico, and Central and South America, now widely naturalized and planted throughout the tropical and subtropical world as an ornamental groundcover. The plant produces prostrate to semi-erect, fleshy stems that root at each node where they contact moist soil, enabling rapid, dense coverage of large areas. The leaves are dark green, coarsely toothed, and slightly succulent. Through the warm months (and year-round in zone 10-12), the plant produces quantities of small (1-inch), bright orange-yellow daisy-like flowers with notched ray petals surrounding a prominent raised central disk — a cheerful, consistent bloom that continues for months. The densely rooting stems form a weed-suppressing mat 6–18 inches deep. Wedelia is exceptionally useful for covering slopes, banks, and erosion-prone areas in zone 9-12 where a tough, low-maintenance groundcover with attractive flowers is desired. It is also widely planted in coastal areas for its salt and wind tolerance. Listed as invasive in many tropical countries — plant responsibly, keep contained, and avoid areas near natural lands. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Sphagneticola trilobata (Wedelia) in Florida landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Wedelia groundcover for Gulf Coast zone 9
LSU AgCenter — Groundcovers for Gulf Coast Louisiana: Wedelia
Invasive Plant Atlas of the United States — Sphagneticola trilobata

common_name: Velvet Arrowhead (Wendland's Syngonium, Silver Goosefoot Plant, Dark Velvet Syngonium)

planting.note: Grow in full shade to partial shade in well-drained, humus-rich soil or on a moss pole for climbing; Syngonium wendlandii is the darkest and most velvety of the commonly cultivated Syngonium species, producing deeply colored, dark forest-green leaves with a striking white central stripe and a velvety texture from tiny, light-scattering surface hairs. It is an outstanding indoor or sheltered outdoor shade plant for zone 10-12 or a protected outdoor position in zone 9b. Keep from temperatures below 50°F. The leaves are beautiful when backlit. CAUTION: all Syngonium parts contain calcium oxalate crystals and are toxic if ingested by pets or children — toxic to cats and dogs.

directions: Grow in full to partial shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil — Velvet Arrowhead is one of the most shade-adapted and visually distinctive of the Syngonium species and thrives in the low-light conditions of a shaded porch, bright interior room, or understory garden in zone 10-12. Syngonium wendlandii is a climbing, rhizomatous tropical vine in the Araceae family native to Costa Rica and Central America, one of the most sought-after species in the genus for the extraordinary visual quality of its leaves. The leaves are a deep, saturated dark forest-green on their upper surfaces, with a broad, brilliant white to silver-white midrib and primary veins creating a dramatic two-tone pattern of dark and light. The surface of the leaf has a distinctly velvety texture from tiny surface papillae that scatter light in a way reminiscent of velvet fabric — making the leaves appear almost three-dimensional, like cut velvet or dark plush. Juvenile plants produce smaller, arrowhead-shaped leaves on compact, low-growing stems. As the plant matures and begins to climb a support, the leaves take on a larger, more broadly lobed form up to 10–12 inches across, with the white markings becoming even more pronounced. Syngonium wendlandii is one of the classic collector aroids now widely appreciated both as a houseplant and as a shaded outdoor container or landscape plant in frost-free climates. The deep shade tolerance, compact juvenile form, and extraordinary leaf pattern make it ideal for shade gardens and sheltered patios. CAUTION: toxic to pets if ingested — keep away from cats and dogs.
sources: American Orchid Society — Syngonium wendlandii cultivation notes
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Syngonium wendlandii species profile
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Syngonium (toxic to cats and dogs)
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Aroids for Florida shade gardens: Syngonium species
